texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: MEASURING THE 'NON-STOPPING TIMENESS' OF ENDS OF PREVISIBLE SETS
texts.text: MEASURING THE 'NON-STOPPING TIMENESS' OF ENDS OF PREVISIBLE SETS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: JU-YI YEN (1) , (2) AND MARC YOR (3) , (4)
texts.text: JU-YI YEN (1) , (2) AND MARC YOR (3) , (4)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract. In this paper, we propose several 'measurements' of the 'nonstopping timeness' of ends G of previsible sets, such that G avoids stopping times, in an ambiant filtration. We then study several explicit examples, involving last passage times of some remarkable martingales.
texts.text: Abstract. In this paper, we propose several 'measurements' of the 'nonstopping timeness' of ends G of previsible sets, such that G avoids stopping times, in an ambiant filtration. We then study several explicit examples, involving last passage times of some remarkable martingales.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction: About ends of previsible sets
texts.text: 1. Introduction: About ends of previsible sets

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we are interested in random times G defined on a filtered probability space (Ω , F , ( F t ) , P ) as ends of ( F t ) previsible sets Γ, that is:
texts.text: In this paper, we are interested in random times G defined on a filtered probability space (Ω , F , ( F t ) , P ) as ends of ( F t ) previsible sets Γ, that is:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: G ≡ G Γ = sup { t : ( t, ω ) ∈ Γ } (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For simplicity, we shall make the following assumptions:
texts.text: For simplicity, we shall make the following assumptions:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (C): All (( F t ) , P ) martingales are continuous;
texts.text: (C): All (( F t ) , P ) martingales are continuous;

texts.label: text
texts.orig: [(C) stands for 'continuous', and (A) for 'avoiding'].
texts.text: [(C) stands for 'continuous', and (A) for 'avoiding'].

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: (A): For any ( F t ) stopping time T , P ( G = T ) = 0.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To such a random time, one associates the Az´ ema supermartingale:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: which, under (CA), admits a continuous version.
texts.text: which, under (CA), admits a continuous version.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In a number of questions, it is very interesting to consider the smallest filtration ( F ′ t ) t ≥ 0 , which contains ( F t ), and makes G a stopping time; this filtration is usually denoted ( F G t ) t ≥ 0 . One of the interests of ( Z G t ) is that it allows to write any ( F t ) martingale as a semimartingale in ( F G t ) t ≥ 0 ; see e.g. [2, 3, 8, 9], for both general formulae and many examples.
texts.text: In a number of questions, it is very interesting to consider the smallest filtration ( F ′ t ) t ≥ 0 , which contains ( F t ), and makes G a stopping time; this filtration is usually denoted ( F G t ) t ≥ 0 . One of the interests of ( Z G t ) is that it allows to write any ( F t ) martingale as a semimartingale in ( F G t ) t ≥ 0 ; see e.g. [2, 3, 8, 9], for both general formulae and many examples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, it has been understood that Black-Scholes like formulae are closely related with certain such G 's, thus throwing a new light on a cornerstone of Mathematical Finance, see, e.g. [6, 7]. In the present paper, with (A) as our essential hypothesis, we would like to measure 'how much G differs from a ( F t ) stopping time'. The remainder of this paper consists in two sections: - In Section 2, we propose several criterions to measure the NST ( ≡ Non Stopping Timeness) of G 's which satisfy (CA); it is not surprising that we get interested in the function:
texts.text: Recently, it has been understood that Black-Scholes like formulae are closely related with certain such G 's, thus throwing a new light on a cornerstone of Mathematical Finance, see, e.g. [6, 7]. In the present paper, with (A) as our essential hypothesis, we would like to measure 'how much G differs from a ( F t ) stopping time'. The remainder of this paper consists in two sections: - In Section 2, we propose several criterions to measure the NST ( ≡ Non Stopping Timeness) of G 's which satisfy (CA); it is not surprising that we get interested in the function:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: - In Section 3, we compute explicitly this function m G for various examples, where G is the last passage time at a level of a martingale which converges to 0, as t →∞ .
texts.text: - In Section 3, we compute explicitly this function m G for various examples, where G is the last passage time at a level of a martingale which converges to 0, as t →∞ .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Several possible 'NST' criterions
texts.text: 2. Several possible 'NST' criterions

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2.1. A fundamental function: m G ( t ) . Let us consider several possible criterions: first of all, if G were a ( F t ) stopping time, then the process 1 ( G≥ t ) would be identically equal to Z G t ≡ P ( G ≥ t |F t ). Thus, the function ( m G ( t ) , t ≥ 0) tells us about the NST of G . Next, a simple but useful remark is that:
texts.text: 2.1. A fundamental function: m G ( t ) .
